title: MCP Integration — MCP for You and Me

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you'll enable the GitHub MCP server in VS Code, verify the connection, work GitHub Issues from Copilot Chat, browse the MCP registry, and install a pre-built MCP server.

The guide panel will update automatically as you advance through the slides. The glowing edge and arrow will show you where to look.

steps.title: Discover MCP Commands
steps.instruction:
Let's see the architecture in action. We've opened the Command Palette for you. Type MCP to filter the commands.

You should see commands like MCP: List Servers, MCP: Add Server, and MCP: Start Server. These map directly to the three layers on the slide: VS Code is the Host, the MCP commands manage the Client, and each server entry is a Server process.

steps.title: List Active MCP Servers
steps.instruction:
We've opened the Command Palette again. Type MCP: List Servers and press Enter.

You should see the GitHub MCP server listed with a running status. This confirms that VS Code (the host) has an active client connection to at least one server process. In later steps we will add a second server so you can see multiple connections side by side.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Command Palette for you.

For future reference:
• Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P — access MCP commands, VS Code settings, and every other command.

steps.title: Explore MCP Settings
steps.instruction:
Open Settings (Ctrl+,) and type mcp in the search box.

Browse the available settings. Look for Chat > MCP: Discovery and any settings related to tool approval or confirmation. These settings control how sampling and elicitation behave: whether tools auto-approve or prompt you first.

steps.title: Test Tool Confirmation
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat (Ctrl+Shift+I) and ask it to do something that requires a write action:

Create a new issue titled "MCP Lab Test" in this repo

Watch for the confirmation dialog before the tool executes. This is elicitation in practice: VS Code asks for your explicit approval before the MCP server takes an action. Click Cancel so the test issue isn't actually created.
steps.tip: Tool confirmation is the safety net that makes MCP practical for production use. Sampling and elicitation ensure the AI never takes actions you haven't approved.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed Settings and Copilot Chat for you.

For future reference:
• Settings: Ctrl+,
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Open VS Code Settings
steps.instruction:
Let's enable the GitHub MCP server. Open Settings by pressing Ctrl+,.

In the search box at the top, type chat.mcp to filter the settings.

steps.title: Enable MCP Support
steps.instruction:
Find the setting Chat > MCP: Enabled and make sure it is checked.

This tells VS Code to allow MCP servers to connect to Copilot Chat. Without this setting, MCP tools will not appear in the chat.
steps.tip: MCP is enabled by default in recent versions of VS Code. If the setting is already checked, you're all set.

steps.title: Reload VS Code
steps.instruction:
If you changed any settings, reload VS Code for the changes to take effect.

Use Ctrl+Shift+P and type Reload Window to restart without closing the application.

steps.title: Open Copilot Chat
steps.instruction: Open Copilot Chat by pressing Ctrl+Shift+I. The chat panel should appear on the side. When MCP tools are available, Copilot Chat operates in agent mode, meaning it can autonomously call tools to complete multi-step tasks.

steps.title: Check for MCP Tools
steps.instruction:
In the chat input area, look for a tools icon (wrench or puzzle piece). Click it to see the list of available MCP tools.

You should see tools from the GitHub MCP server, such as:

• create_issue
• list_issues
• search_code
• add_issue_comment

If you see these, the GitHub MCP server is connected successfully!

steps.title: Troubleshoot If Needed
steps.instruction:
If no tools are showing, we've opened the Output panel for you. Select GitHub MCP from the dropdown at the top-right of the panel. This panel logs connection events and errors.

Common fixes:
• Make sure you're signed in to GitHub
• Verify chat.mcp.enabled is checked
• Try reloading the window again
steps.tip: The Output panel is your best friend for debugging MCP connectivity. Check it first whenever something isn't working.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Output panel and Copilot Chat for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Output Panel: Ctrl+Shift+U — select GitHub MCP from the dropdown to see tool call logs.

steps.title: List Open Issues
steps.instruction:
In Copilot Chat, type the following prompt:

What are the open issues in this repo?

Watch Copilot call the list_issues MCP tool. It will fetch data from the GitHub API and format the results for you.

steps.title: Filter by Label
steps.instruction:
Try a more targeted query. Type:

Show me issues labeled 'bug'

Copilot uses the same MCP tool but passes the label filter. Notice how natural language maps to structured API calls behind the scenes.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed Copilot Chat for you.

Quick reference: Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Comment on an Issue
steps.instruction:
Pick any open issue from the previous results. Ask Copilot to add a comment:

Add a comment to issue #[number] saying "Testing MCP integration from VS Code"

Copilot will show you a confirmation dialog before taking the write action. Review what it's about to do and approve it.
steps.tip: Write actions require your confirmation by default. Copilot will never modify a GitHub resource without your explicit approval. You can configure auto-approval for trusted tools in your settings.

steps.title: Verify the Comment
steps.instruction:
After approving the action, check that the comment was posted. You can ask:

Show me the comments on issue #[number]

Alternatively, open the issue in your browser to verify the comment appears.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed Copilot Chat for you.

Quick reference: Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Search for Code
steps.instruction:
Let's try the code search tool. In Copilot Chat, type:

Search for files that contain "slide-deck" in this repo

Copilot calls the search_code MCP tool and returns matching files with line numbers and code snippets.

steps.title: Combine Search With Reasoning
steps.instruction:
Now combine search with Copilot's reasoning. Try:

Find all HTML files that use speaker notes and tell me which ones are missing them

Copilot searches the code, analyzes the results, and gives you an intelligent summary. This is MCP + LLM reasoning working together.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed Copilot Chat for you.

Quick reference: Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Discover Servers From VS Code
steps.instruction:
You don't need to leave the editor to find MCP servers. We've opened the Command Palette for you. Type MCP: Add Server and press Enter.

Select HTTP (Server-Sent Events) or browse the list. This is VS Code's built-in interface for connecting to MCP servers from the registry. Press Escape to close the dialog for now.

steps.title: Pick a Server to Install
steps.instruction:
We've opened the Command Palette again. Type MCP: Add Server and press Enter. This time, choose Command (stdio), then type:

npx

When prompted for arguments, enter:

-y,@anthropic-ai/mcp-server-fetch

Give it the name fetch. When asked where to save, choose Workspace Settings. This installs the official MCP Fetch server, which Copilot can use to read web pages.
steps.tip: The Fetch server is a great first install because it needs no credentials. It adds a tool that lets Copilot retrieve content from URLs you provide.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Command Palette for you.

Quick reference: Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P

steps.title: Inspect the Configuration File
steps.instruction:
The previous step created a .vscode/mcp.json file (or updated .vscode/settings.json) in your workspace. Open it now to see what VS Code generated.

Compare it with the example on the slide. Notice the structure: a servers map, a command to start the process, and args passed to it. This is the same format you would write by hand.
steps.tip: The .mcp.json at the workspace root is the recommended portable option for sharing with your team. VS Code also supports server configs in .vscode/mcp.json and user settings.

steps.title: Verify the New Server's Tools
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat (Ctrl+Shift+I) and click the tools icon (wrench) in the chat input area.

Scroll through the tool list. Alongside the GitHub tools you saw earlier, you should now see a fetch tool from the server you just installed. If it doesn't appear, reload the window (Ctrl+Shift+P → Reload Window) and check again.

steps.title: Use the New Server
steps.instruction:
Let's confirm it works. In Copilot Chat, type:

Use the fetch tool to get the content of https://modelcontextprotocol.io and summarize what MCP is

Copilot calls the fetch server to retrieve the page, then summarizes the content. You now have two MCP servers running side by side: GitHub for issues and code, Fetch for web content.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed Copilot Chat for you.

Quick reference: Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Audit Your MCP Setup
steps.instruction:
Now that you have two servers running, let's apply the security practices from the slide. We've opened the Command Palette for you. Type MCP: List Servers and press Enter.

For each server, confirm you know:
• Where it came from (official GitHub extension vs. registry)
• What permissions it has (GitHub token vs. no credentials)
• Whether it runs locally (stdio) or remotely (HTTP)

This is the least-privilege audit you should do any time you add a new server.

steps.title: Monitor a Live Tool Call
steps.instruction:
We've opened the Output panel for you. Select GitHub MCP from the dropdown at the top-right. Leave it visible.

Now switch to Copilot Chat and ask:

How many open issues does this repo have?

Switch back to the Output panel and find the list_issues tool call in the log. You can see the exact request and response. This is how you verify that MCP servers are only doing what you expect.
steps.tip: Make checking the Output panel a habit when testing new MCP servers. It shows every tool call, including the parameters sent and data returned.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Output panel and Copilot Chat for you.

For future reference:
• Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Output Panel: Ctrl+Shift+U

steps.title: Start the Demo Workflow
steps.instruction:
It's demo time! Open Copilot Chat and start a new conversation.

Step 1: Ask Copilot to list open issues:

List the open issues in this repository

steps.title: Summarize an Issue
steps.instruction:
Step 2: Pick an issue from the results and ask for a summary:

Summarize issue #[number] including any comments

Copilot fetches the full issue details and discussion thread, then provides a concise summary.

steps.title: Find Related Code
steps.instruction:
Step 3: If the issue mentions any code, ask Copilot to find it:

Find code related to the problem described in that issue

The MCP code search tool will locate relevant files and bring them into the conversation context.

steps.title: Complete the Workflow
steps.instruction:
Step 4-5: Wrap up the workflow:

• Ask Copilot to add a comment to the issue with your findings
• If appropriate, ask Copilot to close the issue

You've now completed an end-to-end issue workflow entirely from within VS Code!
steps.tip: In a real workflow, you'd fix the code, push a commit, and link it to the issue before closing. The demo shows the MCP interaction pattern.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed Copilot Chat for you.

Quick reference: Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations, you've completed the MCP lab! Here's what you accomplished:

• Explored MCP commands and listed active servers from the Command Palette
• Configured MCP settings and tested tool confirmation firsthand
• Enabled the GitHub MCP server and verified the connection
• Listed, filtered, and commented on GitHub Issues from Copilot Chat
• Searched code across repos with the code search tool
• Installed the Fetch MCP server from the registry using the built-in wizard
• Inspected the generated configuration file and used the new server
• Audited your MCP setup and monitored live tool calls in the Output panel
• Completed an end-to-end issue workflow

Next up: Prompts, Instructions, Skills, and Agents to master Copilot customization, or MCP Maximizer to build your own custom MCP servers.
